name: Long Beach Unified School District

schools: California Academy of Mathematics and Science (CAMS)
Sato Academy of Mathematics and Science
calendarExceptions.note: These schools publish a separate calendar. Use the campus-specific version rather than the general district calendar.

districtFact: Long Beach Unified is a founding partner in the Long Beach College Promise — a joint commitment with CSU Long Beach and Long Beach City College that provides eligible LBUSD graduates with college-transition support, two years of tuition-free enrollment at Long Beach City College, and local-preference admission consideration at CSU Long Beach.

about.title: About Long Beach Unified School District
about.content: Long Beach Unified serves approximately 61,000 students across 83 schools in Long Beach, Lakewood, Signal Hill, and Avalon. The district's campuses run from the east-side neighborhoods near Naples and Belmont Shore through Signal Hill — a separate city entirely surrounded by Long Beach — to the Poly and Jordan high school zones that cover some of the denser western and central corridors. Many newcomers assume a Long Beach mailing address automatically means LBUSD, but district boundaries don't follow the city line. Nearby addresses can fall into LAUSD, ABC Unified, Paramount Unified, or Compton Unified depending on the specific street. The school finder at lbschools.net identifies the assigned campus by address.

about.title: What to Know Before the First School Year
about.content:
One thing that consistently surprises families moving from LAUSD or other LA County districts: LBUSD spring break falls in early April, typically a week or two after LAUSD's late-March recess. The two breaks rarely coincide, which matters for parents whose workplace schedules follow a neighboring district's calendar or for households managing kids across both districts.

The Long Beach College Promise connects LBUSD to CSU Long Beach and Long Beach City College through a formal program providing eligible graduates with college-transition support, two years of tuition-free enrollment at LBCC, and local-preference admission consideration at CSULB. CSULB applicants must still meet the university's admission requirements. Many LBUSD families first encounter the College Promise in high school, but knowing the requirements earlier makes course planning a lot more straightforward. Details are at lbschools.net.

content: LBUSD high school graduation ceremonies typically take place in late May or early June, several weeks before the general last day of school for other grades. Parents of seniors should check the specific graduation schedule rather than the district's general end-of-year date.

School Choice and magnet applications don't all open at the same time. High school specialty programs typically open earlier than general elementary enrollment. The grade-specific School Choice page at lbschools.net has current-cycle deadlines.
Campus schedules may include minimum days and conference periods that don't appear in the district's main calendar view. Your school's own page within lbschools.net is where to find those.

districtFaqs.q: Why does LBUSD spring break fall later than LAUSD's?
districtFaqs.a: The two districts set their calendars independently. In recent years, LBUSD spring break has landed in early April while LAUSD's has been in late March — a gap of roughly one to two weeks, though the exact difference shifts each year. Parents moving from LAUSD often don't expect this. Check both districts' published calendars for the specific school year before making travel or childcare arrangements.

districtFaqs.q: Do CAMS and Sato Academy follow the same calendar as other LBUSD schools?
districtFaqs.a: No. CAMS and Sato Academy publish a separate school calendar with different dates. The general district calendar on lbschools.net does not apply to those two schools. Families at CAMS or Sato should download the campus-specific calendar from the same page.

districtFaqs.q: Is my Long Beach mailing address automatically in LBUSD?
districtFaqs.a: Not always. LBUSD serves schools in Long Beach, Lakewood, Signal Hill, and Avalon, but the district boundary doesn't follow the city line. Some Long Beach addresses fall into LAUSD, Compton Unified, or Paramount Unified, and some Signal Hill or Lakewood addresses are in LBUSD. Enter your home address in the school finder at lbschools.net to confirm which campus is assigned.

districtFaqs.q: What is the Long Beach College Promise?
districtFaqs.a: The Long Beach College Promise is a partnership among LBUSD, Long Beach City College, CSU Long Beach, and the City of Long Beach. Eligible LBUSD graduates may receive two years of tuition-free enrollment at LBCC, college-transition support, and local-preference admission consideration at CSULB. CSULB applicants must still meet the university's applicable admission requirements. Reviewing the program before ninth grade allows course planning to stay aligned with college goals. Details are at lbschools.net.

livingHere.intro: Long Beach sits at the edge of the LA basin where the city meets the ocean — lower density than central LA, with a working port on one side and beach neighborhoods on the other. The district serves communities across Long Beach, Lakewood, Signal Hill, and Avalon, and the day-to-day experience of school life here is shaped by that range.

highlights.label: Specialized School Pathways
highlights.detail: CAMS, Sato Academy, and programs like Millikan COMPASS consistently attract more applicants than available seats. Families often end up comparing CAMS and Millikan COMPASS during the application process — the two attract similar interest and their timelines overlap. Parents interested in these pathways tend to start the School Choice process in the fall before they want their child to enroll. High school specialty applications typically open before the general elementary enrollment window. CAMS and Sato also follow a separate calendar, so siblings at different campuses won't always share the same no-school days.

highlights.label: The I-710 Corridor
highlights.detail: The I-710 carries substantial port and freight traffic between the Ports of Long Beach and Los Angeles and the inland distribution network. Families whose school route uses the corridor should check morning travel conditions and allow extra time during heavy traffic periods.

highlights.label: Coastal Climate and Morning Arrival
highlights.detail: Coastal mornings in Long Beach are often noticeably cooler and cloudier than the inland LA basin. A marine layer — low coastal fog — is common through late morning during spring and summer, a pattern locals call 'June Gloom' even though it extends well into July most years. Students may need a light layer at morning drop-off even when afternoons warm up considerably.

highlights.label: Morning Drop-Off and Campus Logistics
highlights.detail: Drop-off timing at LBUSD elementary schools is worth scouting before the first week. Many neighborhood campuses have limited curb space and open access shortly before the bell — arriving at the tail end of the window means longer waits. Older campuses in established neighborhoods tend to have tighter drop-off logistics than newer developments. Poly High and Wilson High both see significant afternoon congestion near campus at dismissal.

highlights.label: LA Metro Access
highlights.detail: The A Line (Blue Line) connects Long Beach to downtown Los Angeles. Parents relying on Metro or Long Beach Transit for older students should check campus-specific dismissal times, particularly on minimum days and conference days when schedules differ from the regular calendar.

relatedDistricts.name: Los Angeles Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: LAUSD serves nearby communities and is the most common comparison for Long Beach families. The two districts publish separate calendars with different start dates and break schedules — worth checking directly for each school year rather than assuming they track together.

relatedDistricts.name: ABC Unified School District
relatedDistricts.reason: ABC Unified serves Cerritos, Artesia, Lakewood, and Hawaiian Gardens, communities directly adjacent to LBUSD's eastern boundary. Households near that border should confirm which district their specific address falls in.
